ai_agent.role: Inspections and permitting specialist managing 29 inspection types across multiple counties, coordinating county-specific scheduling requirements, and tracking hold types that gate construction progress

core_knowledge.description: Master all 29 inspection types required for residential construction in Florida. These span foundation (footer, slab, monolithic), structural (framing, tie-down, roof nailing), mechanical (rough plumbing, rough electrical, rough HVAC, gas line), insulation, drywall, and final inspections (final plumbing, final electrical, final mechanical, final building). Each has specific prerequisites and required documentation.

core_knowledge.topic: Hold Types: Inspection Hold vs Final Hold
core_knowledge.description: Critical distinction: Inspection Hold blocks ALL construction activity on the house until resolved. Final Hold allows construction to continue but blocks Certificate of Occupancy (CO). Incorrectly classifying a hold type can either halt construction unnecessarily or allow work to proceed that must be torn out later.

Each county has completely different inspection scheduling procedures. Marion County requires email requests (inspector responsiveness varies). Citrus County uses an online portal with submissions required by 15:30 the day before. Charlotte County has no portal or email; inspections must be scheduled in person at the site. These differences require maintaining parallel workflows.

Impact fees must be paid before Final Electric inspection can be scheduled. This is a common blocker (PP-18) because the fee payment is handled by a different department and there is no automatic notification when it is paid. Dandara must manually check fee status before scheduling Final Electric.

Understand the full permit lifecycle: application preparation, submission to county, plan review process, revision requests, permit issuance, and permit expiration tracking. Each county has different review timelines (Marion: 2-4 weeks, Citrus: 1-3 weeks, Charlotte: 3-5 weeks).

Dandara also handles utility connections (water, sewer, electric, gas). Each utility company has different lead times, application processes, and connection scheduling requirements. Utility availability can affect construction timeline significantly.

Tribal knowledge about which county inspectors are responsive, which ones require follow-up calls, and which ones have preferred communication methods. This knowledge directly affects how far in advance inspections must be requested to ensure timely scheduling.

When an inspection fails, understand the correction verification process, re-inspection request procedures (county-specific), and priority scheduling to minimize delay. Some counties allow same-day re-inspection for minor items; others require full reschedule.

daily_workflows.name: Daily Inspection Scheduling
steps: Review houses ready for inspection (from Supervisao Checklist Done + Scheduling notifications)
Identify inspection type needed for each house
Check county-specific scheduling requirements
Marion County: send email to inspector with house details and requested inspection type
Citrus County: submit via online portal before 15:30 cutoff
Charlotte County: coordinate site visit with inspector directly
Record scheduled inspection date and type in platform
Notify Scheduling and Supervisao of upcoming inspections
critical_decisions: Whether all prerequisites are met before requesting inspection
Priority ordering when multiple houses need inspections on same day
Whether to request next-day inspection or allow buffer for corrections

daily_workflows.name: Inspection Result Processing
steps: Receive inspection results from county (email, portal, or site report)
Record pass/fail status in platform
For passed inspections: update house status and notify Scheduling (H-08)
For failed inspections: document correction items required
Notify Supervisao of failed items for correction coordination (H-14)
Schedule re-inspection once corrections are confirmed complete
Update hold status if applicable
critical_decisions: Whether failed items require Inspection Hold or can proceed as Final Hold
Whether corrections need engineering review before re-inspection

daily_workflows.name: Hold Management
steps: Monitor all active holds across all houses
Check for hold resolution conditions (corrections made, fees paid, permits issued)
Update hold status when conditions are met
Notify Scheduling that hold is removed so construction can resume
Note: No automatic notification when holds are removed (PP-17)
critical_decisions: Whether to remove hold based on partial correction completion
Whether hold type should be escalated from Final Hold to Inspection Hold

daily_workflows.name: Permit Application & Tracking
steps: Receive permit request from Scheduling or Engineering
Prepare permit application with required documentation
Submit to county building department (method varies by county)
Track plan review status
Respond to revision requests from county reviewer
Record permit issuance date and permit number
Monitor permit expiration dates for active projects
Notify Scheduling when permit is issued (phase unlock trigger)
critical_decisions: Whether permit revision request requires engineering involvement
Whether to appeal a permit denial or modify plans

daily_workflows.name: Impact Fee Tracking for Final Electric
steps: Check which houses are approaching Final Electric inspection stage
Verify impact fee payment status with Financeiro
If fees not paid: escalate to Financeiro with urgency (blocks Final Electric)
Once fees confirmed paid: schedule Final Electric inspection
Document fee payment date for audit trail
critical_decisions: Whether to escalate unpaid impact fees to management
Whether delay in fee payment requires rescheduling other final inspections

daily_workflows.name: Utility Connection Coordination
steps: Submit utility connection applications (water, sewer, electric, gas)
Track application status with each utility company
Schedule meter installations and final connections
Coordinate utility inspections (separate from county building inspections)
Verify utility connection before Certificate of Occupancy request
critical_decisions: Whether utility company lead time affects construction schedule
Whether temporary power/water is needed during construction

features_used: Hold type management (Inspection Hold vs Final Hold)

handoffs.data_exchanged: Pass/fail status, inspection type, correction items if failed, re-inspection date if applicable, hold type placed
handoffs.impact: Passed inspections unlock next phase activities in Scheduling. Failed inspections block scheduling and require correction coordination. This is the primary gate between construction phases.

handoffs.data_exchanged: Failed inspection items, correction requirements, which sub is responsible for corrections, re-inspection timeline
handoffs.impact: Supervisao must coordinate with sub crews to make corrections before re-inspection can be scheduled. Delay in communication delays the correction and re-inspection cycle.

handoffs.data_exchanged: House ID, inspection type needed, supervisor pre-check confirmation, completion date
handoffs.impact: Dandara cannot schedule inspections without knowing which houses are ready. Late notification from Supervisao means missed scheduling windows (especially Citrus County 15:30 cutoff).

handoffs.data_exchanged: Fee payment confirmation, receipt number, house ID
handoffs.impact: Final Electric inspection cannot be scheduled until impact fees are confirmed paid (PP-18). No automatic notification exists, requiring manual checking.

handoffs.data_exchanged: Hold removal confirmation, house ID, which activities are now unblocked
handoffs.impact: No automatic notification when holds are removed (PP-17). Scheduling may not know construction can resume, causing unnecessary idle time.

handoffs.data_exchanged: Permit number, issue date, scope of permitted work, expiration date
handoffs.impact: Permit issuance enables scheduling of permitted work activities. Delay in communication means scheduling cannot plan ahead.

pain_points.description: No automatic notification when a hold is removed from a house. Scheduling and Supervisao must manually check hold status, leading to idle time where construction could resume but nobody knows the hold has been lifted.
pain_points.current_workaround: Dandara sends WhatsApp messages when she remembers to notify, but with 29 inspection types across dozens of houses, notifications are frequently missed or delayed
pain_points.proposed_solution: Automated hold removal notification system that pushes alerts to Scheduling and Supervisao when any hold status changes, with configurable notification preferences per department

pain_points.description: Impact fees block Final Electric inspection, but there is no integration between fee payment tracking (Financeiro) and inspection scheduling (Inspecao). Dandara must manually check with Financeiro whether fees are paid before scheduling Final Electric.
pain_points.current_workaround: Weekly manual check with Financeiro on fee payment status for all houses approaching Final Electric stage; sometimes fees are paid but notification takes days
pain_points.proposed_solution: Direct integration between payment system and inspection scheduling; auto-flag houses eligible for Final Electric when impact fee payment is confirmed

pain_points.description: Single point of failure: Dandara is the sole person handling all inspections AND utilities across all counties. No backup, no cross-training, no documentation of county-specific procedures.
pain_points.current_workaround: When Dandara is absent, inspections are not scheduled and construction stalls on houses awaiting inspection
pain_points.proposed_solution: Cross-train at least one additional person on inspection procedures; document all county-specific workflows; create automated scheduling rules for routine inspections

pain_points.description: County portal inconsistency: each county has a different system (email, portal, in-person) requiring Dandara to maintain multiple workflows simultaneously with no unified interface
pain_points.current_workaround: Dandara maintains mental model of which county uses which system and switches between them throughout the day
pain_points.proposed_solution: Unified inspection request interface in platform that auto-routes to correct county system (API integration where portal exists, email template where email is used)

pain_points.description: No visibility into inspector availability or scheduling conflicts at the county level; inspections are requested without knowing if the inspector has capacity on the requested date
pain_points.current_workaround: Dandara has learned county inspector patterns through experience (tribal knowledge) and schedules accordingly
pain_points.proposed_solution: Historical data analysis of inspection scheduling patterns to predict inspector availability; buffer day recommendations by county

tribal_knowledge.description: Dandara knows the typical response times and scheduling patterns for each county inspector. For example, Marion County inspectors are typically available within 2 days for routine inspections but may take a week for specialty inspections. Citrus County is faster (next-day for routine) but strictly enforces the 15:30 cutoff. Charlotte County inspectors have unpredictable schedules.

Individual inspectors have preferred communication methods. Some respond faster to phone calls, others to email, some prefer text. Dandara knows which approach works best for each inspector, which directly affects how quickly inspections can be scheduled.

Impact fee amounts, calculation methods, and payment timing requirements differ by county. Some counties require fees before permit issuance, others before Final Electric, and some have split-payment options. Dandara knows the exact requirements for each county and project type.

tribal_knowledge.topic: Common Fail Patterns by Inspection Type
tribal_knowledge.description: Dandara has observed which inspection types most commonly fail and for which reasons. This allows her to flag potential issues to Supervisao before the inspection, reducing fail rates. For example, rough plumbing inspections frequently fail due to missing cleanouts or improper venting.

tribal_knowledge.topic: Utility Company Lead Times and Contacts
tribal_knowledge.description: Each utility company (Duke Energy, TECO, local water authorities) has different application procedures, lead times, and key contacts. Dandara maintains personal relationships with utility company representatives that expedite connection scheduling.

Some inspection failures fall in gray areas between Inspection Hold and Final Hold. Dandara has learned from experience which failures truly require construction stoppage vs which can be addressed in parallel with ongoing work. Misclassification either stops construction unnecessarily or allows problematic work to be covered.

topics: Inspection sequencing rules (which inspections must pass before others)
Inspection Hold vs Final Hold definitions
When to apply each hold type
Impact of each hold type on construction schedule
Edge cases and gray areas in hold classification
Marion County: email procedures, inspector contacts, timing
Citrus County: portal login, submission deadline (15:30), interface navigation
Charlotte County: site-only scheduling, inspector availability patterns
Other counties: procedures as coverage expands
Utility connection types (water, sewer, electric, gas)
Impact fee types and calculation methods

compliance_requirements.requirement: All required inspections must pass before proceeding to the next construction phase
compliance_requirements.verification: Inspection results recorded in platform; phase unlock requires all prerequisite inspections showing pass status

compliance_requirements.requirement: Inspection Hold must halt all construction activity on the affected house until resolved
compliance_requirements.verification: System blocks scheduling of new activities when Inspection Hold is active; daily log review confirms no work performed during hold

compliance_requirements.requirement: Building permits must be issued before construction begins on any permitted activity
compliance_requirements.verification: Permit number and issue date recorded in platform; scheduling system checks permit status before allowing activity scheduling

compliance_requirements.requirement: Impact fees must be paid before Final Electric inspection can be requested
compliance_requirements.verification: Fee payment confirmation from Financeiro must be on file before Final Electric inspection request is submitted to county

compliance_requirements.requirement: Inspection requests must be submitted within county-specific deadlines
compliance_requirements.verification: Citrus County submissions verified before 15:30 daily; Marion County emails tracked for response; Charlotte County site visits scheduled within county hours

compliance_requirements.requirement: Certificate of Occupancy requires all final inspections passed and all holds removed
compliance_requirements.verification: System checklist verifying all 29 applicable inspections show pass status and no active holds before CO application

compliance_requirements.requirement: Utility connections must be inspected and approved before occupancy
compliance_requirements.verification: Utility inspection results recorded; utility company approval letters on file

key_metrics.target: >80% of inspections pass on first attempt
key_metrics.measurement: Number of first-attempt passes divided by total inspections requested, tracked by inspection type and county

key_metrics.target: Inspection scheduled within 1 business day of Ready for Inspection notification
key_metrics.measurement: Time between Ready for Inspection notification from Supervisao and inspection request submitted to county
Days between hold placement and hold removal, tracked by hold type (Inspection Hold vs Final Hold)

key_metrics.target: Permit issued within county-standard timeline (varies by county)
key_metrics.measurement: Days between permit application submission and permit issuance, tracked by county and permit type
Number of re-inspections divided by total inspections, tracked by inspection type to identify systemic issues

key_metrics.metric: Impact Fee to Final Electric Lag
key_metrics.target: Final Electric scheduled within 2 business days of impact fee payment confirmation
key_metrics.measurement: Days between fee payment confirmation and Final Electric inspection scheduling

key_metrics.target: 100% of hold changes communicated to Scheduling within 2 hours
key_metrics.measurement: Time between hold status change and notification sent to Scheduling (currently unmeasured due to PP-17)
